127	  else
128	    buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_NO}${RBYC_DEPOT} not configured — the ${RBYC_PAYOR} must establish the ${RBYC_DEPOT}:"
129	    buh_tt "      " "${RBZ_ONBOARD_PAYOR_HB}"
130	  fi
131	  buh_e
132	  buh_line "This track assumes you have completed ${RBHO_TRACK_FIRST_BUILD} —"
133	  buh_line "the ${RBYC_RELIQUARY} is inscribed and you have ${RBYC_ORDAIN}ed at least"
134	  buh_line "one ${RBYC_VESSEL} on the ${RBYC_TETHERED} pool."
135	  buh_e
136	
137	  if test "${z_has_director}" = "0" || test "${z_has_depot}" = "0"; then
138	    buh_error "Complete the prerequisites above before continuing."
139	    buh_e
140	    buh_tt "Return to start: " "${RBZ_ONBOARD_START_HERE}"
141	    buh_e
142	    return 0
143	  fi
144	
145	  buh_line "Configure this handbook session:"
146	  buh_e
147	  buh_code "   export ${RBYC_HANDBOOK_NAMEPLATE_NAME}=${z_moniker}"
148	  buh_e
149	  buh_line "The ${RBYC_CHARGE} and suite commands below reference the ${RBYC_NAMEPLATE}"
150	  buh_line "by name. The two ${RBYC_VESSEL} names shift mid-track (forge →"
151	  buh_line "airgap), so vessels appear as literals in commands."
152	  buh_e
153	
154	  buh_step_style "Step " " — "
155	
156	  buh_step1 "${RBYC_ENSHRINE} the upstream base"
157	  buh_e
158	  buh_line "The airgap chain starts with ownership. ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} mirrors"
159	  buh_line "an upstream image — here rust:slim-bookworm — into your ${RBYC_DEPOT}"
160	  buh_line "under a content-addressed anchor. Once enshrined, builds pull the"
161	  buh_line "base from your ${RBYC_DEPOT} without touching the public internet."
162	  buh_e
163	  buh_line "The ${z_lk_forge} ${RBYC_VESSEL} declares its upstream base in"

buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_YES}${RBYC_DIRECTOR} credential installed"
  else
    buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_NO}${RBYC_DIRECTOR} credential missing — run:"
    buh_tt "      " "${RBZ_ONBOARD_CRED_DIRECTOR}"
  fi
  if test "${z_has_depot}" = "1"; then
    buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_YES}${RBYC_DEPOT} configured (RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER populated)"
  else
    buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_NO}${RBYC_DEPOT} not configured — the ${RBYC_PAYOR} must establish the ${RBYC_DEPOT}:"
    buh_tt "      " "${RBZ_ONBOARD_PAYOR_HB}"
  fi
  buh_e
  buh_line "This track assumes you have completed ${RBHO_TRACK_FIRST_BUILD} —"
  buh_line "the ${RBYC_RELIQUARY} is inscribed and you have ${RBYC_ORDAIN}ed at least"
  buh_line "one ${RBYC_VESSEL} on the ${RBYC_TETHERED} pool."
  buh_e

  if test "${z_has_director}" = "0" || test "${z_has_depot}" = "0"; then
    buh_error "Complete the prerequisites above before continuing."
    buh_e
    buh_tt "Return to start: " "${RBZ_ONBOARD_START_HERE}"
    buh_e
    return 0
  fi

  buh_line "Configure this handbook session:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   export ${RBYC_HANDBOOK_NAMEPLATE_NAME}=${z_moniker}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "The ${RBYC_CHARGE} and suite commands below reference the ${RBYC_NAMEPLATE}"
  buh_line "by name. The two ${RBYC_VESSEL} names shift mid-track (forge →"
  buh_line "airgap), so vessels appear as literals in commands."
  buh_e

  buh_step_style "Step " " — "

  buh_step1 "${RBYC_ENSHRINE} the upstream base"
  buh_e
  buh_line "The airgap chain starts with ownership. ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} mirrors"
  buh_line "an upstream image — here rust:slim-bookworm — into your ${RBYC_DEPOT}"
  buh_line "under a content-addressed anchor. Once enshrined, builds pull the"
  buh_line "base from your ${RBYC_DEPOT} without touching the public internet."
  buh_e
  buh_line "The ${z_lk_forge} ${RBYC_VESSEL} declares its upstream base in"
  buh_line "its ${RBYC_RBRV} file:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rust:slim-bookworm"
  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=rbi_es/rust-slim-bookworm-5ae2d2ef98:rust-slim-bookworm-5ae2d2ef98"
  buh_e
  buh_line "ORIGIN names where the image comes from; ANCHOR is the locator"
  buh_line "for it inside your ${RBYC_DEPOT}. Run ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} on ${z_lk_forge}:"
  buh_e
  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}" "" " ${z_forge_vessel}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "${RBYC_ENSHRINE} resolves the upstream base for that ${RBYC_VESSEL}"
  buh_line "and writes the locator back into its ${RBYC_RBRV}. Slots whose ANCHOR"
  buh_line "is already populated are HEAD-validated against your ${RBYC_DEPOT} and"
  buh_line "left untouched — re-running is idempotent."
  buh_e

content.subject: Phase 3: Handbook tracks rbhodb + rbhodg add inscribe-then-yoke ceremony
content.description: Add inscribe-then-yoke ceremony to rbhodb_director_bind.sh and rbhodg_director_graft.sh, mirroring the existing pattern in rbhodf_director_first_build.sh (conjure) and rbhoda_director_airgap.sh (airgap). The yoke tabtarget rbw-dY already exists — no new tabtarget needed. Operator should be guided to inscribe a reliquary, then yoke it into the vessel before ordain.

      *) z_bottle_bound=1 ;;
    esac
  fi

  local z_vessel_yoked=0
  if test -f "${z_vessel_rbrv}"; then
    local z_vessel_stamp=""

    test -n "${z_vessel_stamp}" && z_vessel_yoked=1
  fi

  buyy_link_yawp "${RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL}" "Vessel"    "${z_vessel}";   local -r z_lk_vessel="${z_buym_yelp}"

[0mthe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Vessel\Vessel]8;;\[0m[0m image from the project's Dockerfile, and SLSA[0m

[0mnaked. [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bind\Bind]8;;\[0m[0m pins it by content hash; the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m[0m blocks all[0m

[0m[32m [*] [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Director\Director]8;;\[0m[0m credential installed[0m

[1;37mMode mixture — name it explicitly[0m

[0mThe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\pluml]8;;\[0m[0m [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Crucible\Crucible]8;;\[0m[0m in this track pairs a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludged]8;;\[0m[0m[0m
[0m(or [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Conjure\Conjured]8;;\[0m[0m) [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m[0m with a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bind\Bind]8;;\[0m[0m-pinned PlantUML [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m[0m.[0m
[0mTwo different [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Ordain\Ordain]8;;\[0m[0m modes cohabiting in one [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Crucible\Crucible]8;;\[0m[0m is the[0m
[0mexpected shape, not a defect — each [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m[0m carries its own[0m
[0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Vouch\Vouch]8;;\[0m[0m verdict, and a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\Nameplate]8;;\[0m[0m can pin [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmarks]8;;\[0m[0m of[0m
[0mdifferent modes without conflict.[0m

[0mThe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m[0m enforces network policy regardless of how it was[0m

content.text: Audit found additional contradicting claims in the same handbook tracks. Per wrap criterion ("no remaining 'bind/graft don't use reliquary' claims") I should fix them — saying "No Cloud Build" implies no reliquary use. Tight surgical edits.

input.old_string:
buh_line "${RBYC_BIND} mode mirrors an upstream image into your ${RBYC_DEPOT} by"
  buh_line "content hash. The ${RBYC_VESSEL} ${RBYC_RBRV} declares the upstream digest:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=bind"
  buh_code "   RBRV_BIND_IMAGE=docker.io/plantuml/plantuml-server@sha256:cd3d67a..."
  buh_e
  buh_line "${RBYC_ORDAIN} reads the mode from the ${RBYC_RBRV} and routes accordingly."
  buh_line "For ${RBYC_BIND}, that means: pull the digest-pinned image from upstream,"
  buh_line "push it to your ${RBYC_DEPOT} under a ${RBYC_HALLMARK} tag. No Dockerfile."
  buh_line "No ${RBYC_POUCH}. No Cloud Build. The image in your ${RBYC_DEPOT} is"
  buh_line "byte-identical to what upstream published at that digest."
input.new_string:
buh_line "${RBYC_BIND} mode mirrors an upstream image into your ${RBYC_DEPOT} by"
  buh_line "content hash. The ${RBYC_VESSEL} ${RBYC_RBRV} declares the upstream digest:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=bind"
  buh_code "   RBRV_BIND_IMAGE=docker.io/plantuml/plantuml-server@sha256:cd3d67a..."
  buh_e
  buh_line "${RBYC_ORDAIN} reads the mode from the ${RBYC_RBRV} and routes accordingly."
  buh_line "For ${RBYC_BIND}, that means: a Cloud Build job runs skopeo (from your"
  buh_line "${RBYC_RELIQUARY}) to copy the digest-pinned image from upstream into"
  buh_line "your ${RBYC_DEPOT} under a ${RBYC_HALLMARK} tag, then the about steps assemble"
  buh_line "${RBYC_SBOM} and metadata. No project Dockerfile, no ${RBYC_POUCH} —"
  buh_line "the image bytes are upstream's, not yours."

#
# Teaches graft mode using rbev-graft-demo: push a locally-built
# image to the Depot. The Director owns the entire build; SLSA
# cannot vouch for this image. The Vouch verdict reads GRAFTED —
# an explicit signal that provenance stops at the local machine.
# No Dockerfile in the project, no Pouch, no Cloud Build, no
# Nameplate, no Crucible — this vessel exists as teaching contrast
# in the trust hierarchy across the three ordain modes.

content.text:
## Phase 3 complete

**Handbook track edits:**

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `rbhodb_director_bind.sh` | Added `z_vessel_rbrv` + `z_vessel_yoked` probe; new "Yoke rbev-bottle-plantuml to a Reliquary" step between sentry-prep and bind-ordain (auto-renumbered to Step 2); fixed introductory framing ("no Cloud Build" → CB runs skopeo+about/vouch, no image build); fixed bind step prose (CB job runs skopeo from reliquary, then about); fixed wall-clock claim (was "seconds, not minutes"; now ~5-10min for skopeo+metadata) |
| `rbhodg_director_graft.sh` | Added `z_vessel_yoked` probe; new "Yoke rbev-graft-demo to a Reliquary" step between local-build-prep and graft-push (auto-renumbered); fixed file-header comment ("no Cloud Build" → "no Cloud Build for the image itself"); fixed graft step prose (combined about+vouch CB job runs after push); fixed wall-clock claim (was "seconds"; now "a few minutes" with about+vouch following the push); fixed "Why Graft exists" framing (no SLSA but about+vouch metadata still runs on reliquary tools) |

**Verification:**
- bash -n: green on both
- Visual render via `tt/rbw-Odb.OnboardingDirectorBind.sh` confirmed step auto-numbering: Step 1 sentry-prep, Step 2 yoke-vessel (NEW), Step 3 bind ordain, Step 4 vouch, Step 5 charge crucible, Step 6 pattern. The yoke command, inscribe fallback, universal-precondition explanation, and probe all rendered correctly.
- Fast suite: identical baseline (25/26).
- Handbook tree audit: no surviving stale "no Cloud Build" claims tied to bind/graft (remaining mentions are about kludge-local and pre-populated-enshrine HEAD-gate — genuinely correct contexts).

**Decisions:**
- Did the edits directly rather than delegating — handbook prose requires careful fit with surrounding voice and the existing buh_line / yawp / yk patterns; subagent overhead would exceed the surgical-edit cost.
- Within-scope expansion: surfaced "no Cloud Build" claims in the same tracks I was editing and fixed them per wrap criterion's "tell one story" requirement. Did NOT broaden to RBSAA / other tracks where the docket didn't reach.

**Adjacency surfaced (NOT fixed):**
- The orchestration-only auto-yoke for forge/airgap vessels in `rbhoda` means a manual operator following just rbhoda doesn't see an explicit yoke step — they get stamps from rbhodf's yoke fan-out only if rbhodf was the prior track. Could add explicit yoke steps to rbhoda for forge and airgap-bottle vessels for symmetry, but outside docket surface list.

Want me to notch phase 3, then run the empirical wrap-criterion check (ordain-bind + ordain-graft against canonical-established depot)?

message.content: notch first.  Then please now review your bash coding against BCG and assure compliance.

params.intent: BBAA4 reliquary-universal-required phase 3 — handbook tracks. rbhodb (director bind track): added z_vessel_rbrv local resolving to RBRR_VESSEL_DIR/rbev-bottle-plantuml/rbrv.env and z_vessel_yoked probe reading RBRV_RELIQUARY through zrbho_po_extract_capture; inserted new buh_step1 'Yoke rbev-bottle-plantuml to a Reliquary' between Step 1 (sentry prep) and the bind ordain step — buh_step1 auto-numbering renumbered the trailing steps. New step explains the universal-reliquary precondition, points operator at rbhodf if reliquary not inscribed, otherwise just prints the inscribe tabtarget for fresh paths, then walks through RBZ_YOKE_RELIQUARY for the bind vessel; closes with a yes/no probe line. Same shape for rbhodg (director graft track) with z_vessel_yoked probe added alongside the existing z_vessel_ready probe; new step inserted between Step 1 (build local image + set RBRV_GRAFT_IMAGE) and the graft ordain step. Also surgical edits to surrounding stale prose where the wrap criterion's 'tell one story' required them: (a) rbhodb section intro 'no Dockerfile, no build context, no Cloud Build' rewritten to 'no image build — Cloud Build runs skopeo to copy the digest-pinned upstream image into your DEPOT, then about/vouch records what was mirrored'; (b) rbhodb's Bind ordain step prose rewritten from 'No Dockerfile. No POUCH. No Cloud Build' to describe the skopeo+about pipeline accurately; (c) rbhodb wall-clock claim 'seconds, not minutes' fixed to '~5-10 minutes' (mirror is rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh's 100x5s wait window per zrbfc_wait_build_completion); (d) rbhodg file-header comment 'no Cloud Build' qualified to 'no Cloud Build for the image itself (about+vouch metadata still runs on the reliquary)'; (e) rbhodg 'Why Graft exists' section's 'no SBOM from the project's pipeline' rewritten to acknowledge the about+vouch combined CB job that follows the push and records GRAFTED; (f) rbhodg graft ordain step's 'No Cloud Build is triggered' rewritten to describe the combined about+vouch job; (g) rbhodg wall-clock 'seconds' → 'a few minutes — push completes in seconds, combined about+vouch job takes the rest'. BCG audit on edited bash: only constructs touched are local declarations (one per line, mutable for synthesized values per Exception 2), test command (no [[ ]] introduced), braced quoted expansion on all variable references, || die guards on every I/O (fact-file extraction via existing zrbho_po_extract_capture), case dispatch already present, no new bash 4+ features, no heredocs, no inline shellcheck. Verification: bash -n green on both files; tt/rbw-Odb.OnboardingDirectorBind.sh rendered visually — Step auto-numbering correct (1 sentry-prep, 2 yoke-vessel NEW, 3 bind ordain, 4 vouch, 5 charge crucible, 6 pattern), yoke command + inscribe fallback + probe rendered correctly with hyperlinked yelps; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh identical baseline 25/26 (rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels pre-existing fail unchanged from BBAAs). Audit pass: no surviving 'bind/graft don't use Cloud Build/reliquary' claims in rbhodb or rbhodg; remaining 'no Cloud Build' mentions in rbhoda (enshrine pre-populated HEAD-gate, kludge-D local provenance) are genuinely correct contexts and out of pace scope. Adjacency surfaced not fixed: rbhoda lacks explicit yoke steps for forge/airgap-bottle vessels that operators need when running the airgap track without coming from rbhodf first — outside docket surface list, candidate for follow-up. Empirical wrap-criterion runs (ordain-bind, ordain-graft against canonical-established depot) remain in phase 4.

1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	«prefix»_cli.sh (executable entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# ❌ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
85	
86	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
87	
88	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
89	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
90	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
91	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
92	
93	### Regime Module Archetype
94	
95	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
96	
97	| Component | Location | Purpose |
98	|-----------|----------|---------|
99	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
100	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
101	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
102	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
103	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
104	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
105	
106	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
107	
108	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
109	
110	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
111	
112	---
113	
114	## Function Patterns
115	
116	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
117	
118	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
119	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
120	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
121	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
122	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
123	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
124	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
125	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
126	
127	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
128	
129	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
130	
131	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
132	
133	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
134	
135	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
136	
137	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
138	
139	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
140	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
141	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
142	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
143	
144	---
145	
146	## File Templates
147	
148	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
149	
150	```bash
151	#!/bin/bash
152	#
153	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
154	#
155	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
156	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
157	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
158	#
159	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
160	#
161	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
162	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
163	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
164	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
165	# limitations under the License.
166	#
167	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
168	#
169	```
170	
171	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
172	
173	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
174	
175	| Variable | Source | Value |
176	|----------|--------|-------|
177	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
178	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
179	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
180	
181	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
182	
183	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
184	
185	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
186	
187	```bash
188	# «shebang»
189	# «copyright»
190	
191	set -euo pipefail
192	
193	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
194	
195	z«prefix»_furnish() {
196	
197	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
198	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
199	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
200	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
201	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
202	
203	  # Source all dependencies
204	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
205	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
206	
207	  # Source regime files, if any
208	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
209	
210	  z«prefix»_kindle
211	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
212	}
213	
214	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
215	
216	# eof
217	```
218	
219	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
220	
221	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
222	
223	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
224	
225	```bash
226	z«prefix»_furnish() {
227	  local z_command="${1:-}"
228	
229	  # Document environment variables
230	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
231	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
232	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
233	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
234	
235	  # Always source light dependencies
236	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
237	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
238	
239	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
240	  case "${z_command}" in
241	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
242	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
243	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
244	      zheavy_kindle
245	      ;;
246	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
247	      # These commands work with config only
248	      ;;
249	  esac
250

# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference

## Core Philosophy

This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.

The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.

Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.

Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.

The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.

### Load-Bearing Complexity

Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.

This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"

Concrete examples from this codebase:

- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.

Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.

## Module Architecture

Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.

```bash
«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
«prefix»_cli.sh (executable entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
```

### CLI as Module Gateway

**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.

**Pattern**:
```
External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
```

**Why this matters**:
- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality

**Anti-pattern**:
```bash
# ❌ External script kindling directly
source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
rbob_charge
```

**Correct pattern**:
```bash
# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
```

**What counts as "external code"**:
- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
- TabTargets and launchers
- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules

**What can call kindle**:
- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior

### Module-CLI Prefix Unity

**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).

When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
dependencies based on which command is being invoked.

### Regime Module Archetype

A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:

| Component | Location | Purpose |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |

Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.

**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.

Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.

---

## Function Patterns

### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)

| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |

**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.

**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.

**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.

**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.

### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)

This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.

| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |

---

## File Templates

### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
```

### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables

Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():

| Variable | Source | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |

These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.

### Template 2: CLI Entry Point

BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.

```bash
# «shebang»
# «copyright»

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

z«prefix»_furnish() {

  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Source all dependencies
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"

  # Source regime files, if any
  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"

  z«prefix»_kindle
  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
}

buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"

# eof
```

### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)

The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.

**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.

```bash
z«prefix»_furnish() {
  local z_command="${1:-}"

  # Document environment variables
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Always source light dependencies
  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"

  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
  case "${z_command}" in
    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
      zheavy_kindle
      ;;
    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
      # These commands work with config only
      ;;
  esac

256	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
257	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
258	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
259	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
260	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
261

263	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
264	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
265	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
266	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure

386	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
387	
388	### Expansion Requirements
389	
390	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
391	```bash
392	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
393	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
394	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
395	```
396	
397	**Never use:**
398	```bash
399	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
400	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
401	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
402	```
403	
404	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
405	```bash
406	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
407	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
408	
409	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
410	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
411	```
412	
413	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
414	```bash
415	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
416	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
417	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
418	
419	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
420	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
421	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
422	```
423	
424	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
425	
426	### Assignment Patterns
427	
428	```bash
429	# Direct assignment
430	local z_message="Processing item"
431	
432	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
433	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
434	
435	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
436	local z_name=""
437	local z_value="default"
438	local z_result=0
439	
440	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
441	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
442	
443	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
444	local z_count="${z_input}"
445	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
446	
447	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
448	local z_token
449	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
450	
451	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
452	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
453	
454	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
455	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
456	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"
457	
458	# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
459	# ❌ local z_content
460	# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
461	
462	# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
463	local z_token
464	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
465	
466	# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
467	echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
468	complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
469	process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
470	local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
471	test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
472	```
473	
474	### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools
475	
476	```bash
477	# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
478	"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
479	"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
480	"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
481	"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
482	"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'
483	
484	# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
485	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
486	  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
487	done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"
488	
489	# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
490	z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
491	```
492	
493	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
494	
495	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
496	
497	```bash
498	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
499	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
500	
501	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
502	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
503	
504	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
505	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
506	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
507	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
508	
509	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
510	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
511	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
512	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
513	
514	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
515	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
516	```
517	
518	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
519	
520	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
521	
522	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
523	
524	```bash
525	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
526	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
527	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
528	
529	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
530	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
531	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
532	```
533	
534	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
535	
536	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
537	
538	```bash
539	# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
540	Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"
541	
542	# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
543	local z_index=0
544	while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
545	  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
546	  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
547	
548	  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
549	    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \

}
```

**Key points:**
- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies

**Benefits:**
- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure

### Template 3: Implementation Module

```bash
# «shebang»
# «copyright»

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1

# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)

z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps

  # Validate module specific environment
  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present

  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"

  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"

  # Kindle constants (internal)
  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use

  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"

  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}

z«prefix»_sentinel() {
  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
}

# Helper functions follow

######################################################################
# External Functions («prefix»_*)

«prefix»_«command»() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  local z_param1="${1:-}"
  local z_param2="${2:-}"

  # Documentation block
  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Implementation
  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"

  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"

  buc_success "«success_message»"
}
```

```bash

# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
```

### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools

```bash
# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'

# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"

# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
```

### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution

All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!

# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!

# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"

# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"

# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
```

**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.

### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress

Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"

# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
```

**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.

**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:

```bash
# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"

# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
local z_index=0
while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"

  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \

content.content:
550	    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
551	
552	  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
553	done
554	```

557	
558	```bash
559	# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
560	jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
561	grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
562	
563	# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
564	```

566	### Readonly Patterns
567	
568	Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:
569	
570	#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables
571	
572	Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.
573	
574	```bash
575	# ✅ Default: readonly locals
576	local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
577	local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
578	local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
579	local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
580	```
581	
582	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
583	
584	```bash
585	# Exception 1: Loop counters
586	local z_index=0
587	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
588	
589	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
590	local z_manifest_file=""
591	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
592	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
593	  ...
594	done
595	
596	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
597	local z_token
598	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
599	
600	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
601	local z_any_failed=0
602	local z_current_group=""
603	```
604	
605	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
606	
607	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
608	
609	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
610	
611	```bash
612	z«prefix»_kindle() {
613	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
614	
615	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
616	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
617	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
618	
619	  # Computed from already-set values
620	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
621	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
622	
623	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
624	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
625	
626	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
627	}
628	```
629	
630	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
631	
632	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
633	
634	#### Mutable Kindle State
635	
636	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
637	
638	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
639	
640	```bash
641	z«prefix»_kindle() {
642	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
643	
644	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
645	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
646	
647	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
648	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
649	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
650	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
651	
652	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
653	}
654	```
655	
656	**Rules**:
657	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
658	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
659	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
660	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
661	
662	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
663	
664	```bash
665	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
666	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
667	  z«prefix»_sentinel
668	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
669	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
670	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
671	}
672	```
673	
674	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
675	```bash
676	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
677	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
678	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
679	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
680	```
681	
682	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
683	
684	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
685	
686	```bash
687	z«prefix»_enforce() {
688	  z«prefix»_sentinel
689	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
690	  # ... custom format checks ...
691	}
692	
693	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
694	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
695	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
696	
697	# Derived state built from validated values:
698	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
699	```
700	
701	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
702	
703	## Special Function Definitions
704	
705	### Special Function Patterns
706	
707	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
708	
709	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
710	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
711	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
712	| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
713	| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
714	| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
715	| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
716	| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
717	| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |
718	
719	---
720	
721	#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
722	
723	**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
724	
725	Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
726	
727	```bash
728	z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
729	  z«prefix»_sentinel
730	
731	  # Process and validate internally
732	  local z_result
733	  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
734	
735	  # Validate without buc_die
736	  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
737	
738	  # Single output at end
739	  echo "${z_result}"
740	}
741	
742	# Caller uses two-line pattern
743	local z_value
744	z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
745	```
746	
747	**Use capture functions for:**
748	- Secrets that must never touch disk
749	- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
750	- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
751	
752	**Contract:**
753	- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
754	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
755	- No side effects beyond the returned value
756	- May source credential files when necessary for security
757	- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
758	- Never writes secrets to disk
759	- Never writes to stderr
760	
761	#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
762	
763	Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
764	
765	```bash
766	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
767	  z«prefix»_sentinel
768	
769	  local z_file="${1:-}"
770	
771	  # Return status only - no output, no dies
772	  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
773	  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1
774	
775	  return 0
776	}
777	
778	# Caller uses in conditionals
779	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
780	```
781	
782	#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)
783	
784	**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
785	
786	**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
787	- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
788	- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
789	- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
790	- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
791	
792	**Example** (using synthetic notation — «reg» owns the rolls, «con» consumes them):
793	
794	```bash
795	# Module «reg» — owns the rolls
796	z«reg»_kindle() {
797	  test -z "${Z«REG»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
798	
799	  z_«reg»_name_roll=()
800	  z_«reg»_target_roll=()
801	  z_«reg»_handler_roll=()
802	
803	  Z«REG»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last — sentinel guards all subsequent calls
804	}
805	
806	# Enroll function — called by consuming module's kindle, NOT «reg»'s own kindle
807	«reg»_enroll() {
808	  z«reg»_sentinel   # Safe: «reg» is already kindled when consumer calls this
809	
810	  local z_name="${1:-}"
811	  local z_target="${2:-}"
812	  local z_handler="${3:-}"
813	  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"
814	
815	  # Registration-time validation
816	  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
817	
818	  z_«reg»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
819	  z_«reg»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
820	  z_«reg»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")
821	
822	  # Return via variable — NOT echo
823	  z_«reg»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
824	}
825	
826	# Module «con» — consumes the registry
827	z«con»_kindle() {
828	  test -z "${Z«CON»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
829	
830	  # «reg» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
831	  «reg»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
832	  Z«CON»_DEFAULT="${z_«reg»_enroll_name}"
833	
834	  «reg»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"
835	
836	  Z«CON»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
837	}
838	```
839	
840	**Key constraints**:
841	- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
842	- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
843	- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
844	- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
845	- Return values use `z_«funcname»_«retval»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
846	- Must NOT be called inside `$()` — side effects would be lost
847	- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
848	
849	**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.

done
